text: There was a long war between the house of Saul and the house of David; David grew steadily stronger, while the house of Saul grew weaker.
Sons were born to David at Hebron: his firstborn was Amnon, by Ahinoam of Jezreel.
His second was Chileab, by Abigail, Nabal's widow; the third was Absalom, son of Maacah, daughter of Talmai king of Geshur.
The fourth was Adonijah, son of Haggith; the fifth was Shephatiah, son of Abital.
The sixth was Ithream, by David's wife Eglah; these were born to David in Hebron.
During the war between the two houses, Abner was making himself strong in the house of Saul.
Saul had a concubine named Rizpah, daughter of Aiah; and Ish-bosheth asked Abner why he had gone in to his father's concubine.
Abner was very angry at the words and said he was no traitor to Judah, and yet Ish-bosheth was charging him with a fault over this woman.
He swore that he would do for David just as the LORD had promised him.
He vowed to transfer the kingdom from the house of Saul and set up David's throne over Israel and Judah, from Dan to Beersheba.
Ish-bosheth could not answer Abner another word, because he was afraid of him.
Abner sent messengers to David on his behalf, asking whose land it was and proposing a covenant, promising to bring all Israel over to him.
David agreed to make a covenant with him, but required one thing: that Abner bring Michal, Saul's daughter, when he came to see him.
David also sent messengers to Ish-bosheth, demanding his wife Michal, whom he had betrothed for a hundred Philistine foreskins.
Ish-bosheth sent and took her from her husband Paltiel son of Laish.
Her husband went with her, weeping as he followed her to Bahurim, until Abner told him to go back, and he returned.
Abner conferred with the elders of Israel, telling them they had long wanted David as king over them.
He urged them to act now, since the LORD had promised to save Israel by David's hand from the Philistines and all their enemies.
Abner also spoke to Benjamin, then went to tell David at Hebron all that Israel and the house of Benjamin wanted.
Abner came to David at Hebron with twenty men, and David made a feast for Abner and his men.
Abner told David he would go and gather all Israel to his lord the king, so they might make a covenant, and David could reign over all his heart desired; so David sent Abner away in peace.
Just then David's servants and Joab came back from a raid, bringing much spoil, but Abner was no longer with David at Hebron, for David had sent him away in peace.
When Joab and the army with him arrived, he was told that Abner son of Ner had come to the king and been sent away in peace.
Joab went to the king and asked what he had done, since Abner had come to him and been let go without being detained.
Joab said David surely knew that Abner had come to deceive him, to learn his movements and all that he was doing.
Joab left David and sent messengers after Abner, who brought him back from the cistern of Sirah, without David's knowledge.
When Abner returned to Hebron, Joab took him aside inside the gate to speak with him privately, and there struck him in the stomach, so that he died, avenging the blood of Asahel his brother.
Afterward David heard of it and said that he and his kingdom were forever guiltless before the LORD for the blood of Abner son of Ner.
He said the guilt should fall on Joab's head and his father's house, and that his house should never lack someone with a discharge, leprosy, weakness, a fallen sword, or lack of bread.
So Joab and his brother Abishai killed Abner because he had put their brother Asahel to death at Gibeon in the battle.
David told Joab and all the people with him to tear their clothes, put on sackcloth, and mourn before Abner; and King David himself walked behind the funeral bier.
They buried Abner at Hebron; the king lifted up his voice and wept at Abner's grave, and all the people wept too.
The king sang a lament for Abner, asking whether Abner had to die as a fool dies.
His hands were not bound, his feet not put in fetters; he fell as one falls before the wicked — and all the people wept over him again.
All the people came to urge David to eat bread while it was still day, but he swore he would taste nothing until sunset.
All the people took notice of it, and it pleased them, as everything the king did pleased the people.
So all the people and all Israel understood that day that the king had no part in putting Abner son of Ner to death.
The king told his servants that a prince and a great man had fallen that day in Israel.
He said that though he was king, he was weak, and these men, the sons of Zeruiah, were too fierce for him — and prayed the LORD would repay the evildoer for his wickedness.
